meta.name: Marcus Vane
meta.title: Partner, Criminal Defence – Serious & Organised Crime

lawyers.summary: Marcus acts exclusively for defendants facing charges of organised crime, conspiracy, and large-scale drug trafficking. His approach is blunt: identify the prosecution's weakest evidence first, then dismantle it before trial. He has secured acquittals in seven multi-defendant conspiracy trials in the last four years. If you are under investigation or have been charged in a serious organised crime matter, instruct him immediately and say nothing to police.
corpus: You are facing a maximum of life imprisonment. The wiretap evidence disclosed yesterday changes everything. Do not speak to anyone about this case — not friends, not family, not cellmates. Assume every conversation is recorded. I need you in my office Thursday at 9 a.m. sharp. Bring nothing written down. We have a narrow window to challenge the interception warrant and I intend to use it. Do not miss this meeting.

The co-accused has entered a cooperation agreement with the Crown. That is the most dangerous development in your case to date. He knows your movements on the fourteenth. He may know more. Stop contacting his family immediately. Any approach — direct or indirect — will be charged as witness interference and will destroy any bail prospects you have left. Sit tight. Say nothing. I am reviewing his proffer conditions now and will advise you by end of week.

Your financial records have been seized under a production order. The prosecution is building a confiscation case alongside the primary charges. That means your home, your accounts, and your business interests are all exposed. Do not move any assets. Do not transfer, sell, or gift anything to anyone. Doing so constitutes a criminal offence in itself. I am instructing a forensic accountant today. You will hear from me before Friday.

The surveillance footage from the warehouse is admissible. I have reviewed the ruling. We lost that argument. Adjust your expectations accordingly — a trial conviction on count two is now a serious probability. We need to talk about the value of an early guilty plea on the lesser counts and what that buys us at sentencing. This is not a decision I make for you. But you need to make it fast. Sentencing discounts erode the longer you wait. Call me today.

Border Force recovered the consignment. All of it. The street value figures the prosecution will quote to the jury are designed to be inflammatory — and they will work unless we get ahead of them. I am already moving to challenge the chain of custody on the forensic samples. There is also a serious question about the legality of the search. Do not travel internationally under any circumstances. Surrender your passport to my office by close of business tomorrow. No exceptions.

meta.name: Diane Okafor
meta.title: Partner, Criminal Defence – Fraud & Financial Crime

lawyers.summary: Diane defends individuals and corporate officers charged with fraud, money laundering, and financial regulatory offences. She cuts through disclosure bundles fast, spotting procedural failures and evidential gaps that others miss. Her background in accountancy means she interrogates financial schedules at the same level as the prosecution's own experts. If your case involves seized assets, a restraint order, or an SFO dawn raid, she is the first call to make.
corpus:
You are facing federal wire fraud charges. Maximum exposure is 20 years per count. You have seven counts. Do the math.

Stop talking. To anyone. Your brother, your accountant, your golf buddy — none of them. Every conversation is a liability you cannot afford.

Do not delete emails. Do not move files. Do not touch the QuickBooks records. Spoliation will bury you faster than the underlying charges.

Bring every bank statement from the last five years to our meeting Thursday. Every account. Personal, corporate, joint — all of it.

I will tell you where you stand after I see the documents. Not before.

The SEC referral to DOJ is confirmed. This is no longer a civil matter. Criminal exposure is now live.

Your instinct to cooperate fully with the Commission was wrong. I understand why you did it. It does not matter. Those statements exist and prosecutors will use them.

Do not speak to the SEC examiner again. Direct all contact to this office immediately.

I need the complete record of every communication between you and Harmon between January and August. Text messages included. Do not filter what you send me — let me decide what matters.

We have a narrow window to get ahead of this. Use it.

A grand jury subpoena is not an invitation. It is a demand backed by the full weight of federal authority. Ignoring it or responding carelessly will make your situation categorically worse.

You are not the target — today. That can change depending entirely on what you say in that room.

You will not testify without preparation. Full stop. We will meet for no less than three sessions before you go anywhere near that proceeding.

Assert nothing voluntarily. Answer only what is asked. Short answers. Factual answers. If you do not know, say so.

Call my direct line before you speak to the U.S. Attorney's office again. Before. Not after.

Your co-defendant's lawyer called mine. They are exploring a cooperation agreement. You need to understand what that means for you.

If Marcus flips, he becomes the government's most valuable witness. He was present at every key transaction. He has documents you do not know exist.

This changes our calculus. Not necessarily toward cooperation on your end — but we need to run both scenarios hard, starting now.

Do not contact Marcus. Do not contact his wife. Do not send messages through intermediaries. Any attempt to influence his decision is obstruction. That charge carries its own prison term.

Come in Monday morning. Bring nothing digital. We talk in person only from here forward.

The asset freeze order was signed this morning. Your accounts — business and personal — are locked. This is the government's standard move in money laundering prosecutions. It is aggressive. It is also challengeable.

You need operating funds to run the business and to pay legal fees. We will file an emergency motion to release a defined portion of the frozen assets. That motion requires a clean accounting of every dollar in those accounts and its legitimate source.

Start building that paper trail today. Invoices, contracts, payroll records, tax filings — everything that shows lawful origin.

Do not attempt to access the accounts through a third party. Do not ask your spouse to move money. The court order covers all of it and prosecutors are watching.

This is manageable. But only if you move correctly and move now.

meta.name: Jonathan Fairweather
meta.title: Partner, Real Estate – Residential Conveyancing & Property Finance

lawyers.summary: Jonathan guides buyers, sellers, and lenders through every stage of residential property transactions, from initial title investigation through to post-completion registration. He takes a methodical approach to searches, survey reports, and mortgage conditions, ensuring no step is rushed and no condition is overlooked. His team handles leasehold purchases, shared-ownership schemes, and Help-to-Buy completions with equal care. Clients consistently describe the process as clear, well-paced, and free of unwelcome surprises.
corpus:
Dear Mrs. Calloway,

I wanted to reach out at the outset of your purchase to reassure you that, while the conveyancing process can feel rather involved at first glance, it is in fact a well-established sequence of steps that we will navigate together in an orderly and unhurried fashion.

Generally speaking, the first stage involves our office raising a series of standard enquiries with the seller's solicitors — questions that touch on matters such as boundaries, planning history, and any rights of way that may affect the property — and, while it is not unusual for this exchange to take a week or two, I would ask you not to read any delay as a cause for concern, since these enquiries are entirely routine and form a necessary foundation for everything that follows.

Once we have satisfactory replies and the local authority search results have returned — which typically takes between ten days and three weeks depending on the relevant council — we will be in a position to report to you comprehensively on the title and the terms of the draft contract, at which point you and I can speak at whatever length you feel you need.

Please do feel free to reach out at any stage if a question arises, however small it may seem, because I find that clients who stay well-informed throughout the process tend to find the whole experience considerably less stressful, and that is very much the outcome I am aiming for on your behalf.

With warm regards,
Jonathan Fairweather

Dear Mr. and Mrs. Okafor,

Thank you for instructing us in connection with the remortgage of your property on Larkspur Avenue, and I am pleased to confirm that we have now received the formal mortgage offer from your new lender, which is an encouraging milestone and means we are able to move the matter forward in a structured and deliberate way.

As is typical in remortgage transactions, our role here is a dual one — we act both for you as the borrowers and for the lender itself, which is standard practice and simply means that we are required to satisfy certain conditions on the lender's behalf before we can proceed to completion, and I wanted to be transparent about that so there are no surprises along the way.

The principal steps remaining are, broadly, as follows: we will first need to carry out a review of the title register at HM Land Registry to confirm there are no unexpected entries or restrictions, we will then report to the lender on the title in the form they require, and finally, once all parties are satisfied, we will arrange for the existing mortgage to be redeemed simultaneously with the drawing down of the new facility, which we should be able to coordinate smoothly provided the redemption figure from your current lender arrives in good time.

I would anticipate that, all things proceeding normally, we could be in a position to complete within approximately three to four weeks from today, though I will of course keep you updated at each material stage and will never take any step without first confirming your instructions.

Please do not hesitate to contact me or my assistant, Claire, should anything be unclear.

Kind regards,
Jonathan Fairweather

Dear Miss Tran,

I hope this message finds you well, and I am writing today to walk you through the next phase of your leasehold purchase, as I appreciate that leasehold transactions carry a few additional layers that freehold purchases do not, and I want to make sure you feel entirely comfortable with what those layers involve and why they exist.

Generally, where a property is held on a long lease, we as your solicitors will need to review the lease itself with some care — paying particular attention to the length of the term remaining, the ground rent provisions, the service charge arrangements, and any restrictions on use or alteration — and I am pleased to say that on a preliminary review the lease appears to be in relatively standard form, which is reassuring, though I will of course report to you in full once I have completed that analysis.

We will also need to raise what are known as leasehold enquiries with the freeholder or their managing agents, asking them to provide up-to-date information about the service charge accounts, any planned major works to the building, and the buildings insurance arrangements, among other things, and while it is not uncommon for managing agents to take a little time in responding, I will follow up proactively on your behalf to keep things moving at a sensible pace.

I would also gently flag at this stage that your mortgage lender will have its own requirements in relation to the lease — most notably a minimum unexpired term — and since the lease here has approximately one hundred and twelve years remaining, we should be able to satisfy those requirements without difficulty, which is genuinely good news.

I look forward to reporting to you more fully in the coming days, and in the meantime please do not hesitate to call or write if anything comes to mind.

Warmly,
Jonathan Fairweather

Dear Mr. Pemberton,

I am writing to update you on the progress of the sale of your property and, more particularly, to address the matter that has arisen in connection with the title that I mentioned briefly when we last spoke, because I want to be straightforward with you about it while also reassuring you that, in my experience, issues of this nature are typically very manageable and rarely derail a transaction of this kind.

The position, in short, is that the title register discloses a restrictive covenant dating from the original conveyance of the property in 1962, which on its face restricts the use of the land to residential purposes only — a restriction that, in all likelihood, has been observed consistently ever since and therefore presents no practical difficulty, but which the buyer's solicitors have nonetheless quite reasonably raised as a formal enquiry, as any diligent practitioner in their position would.

The most straightforward route to resolving this, and the one I would generally recommend in circumstances such as these, is to obtain indemnity insurance, which is a relatively modest and one-off expense that provides both you and the buyer — and indeed their mortgage lender — with protection against any theoretical future claim under the covenant, and which in practice can typically be arranged within a matter of days once we have submitted the relevant application to an insurer.

I will, with your authority, proceed to obtain indicative terms from one or two specialist providers so that we can present a solution to the buyer's solicitors promptly and without unnecessary delay to your anticipated completion date, which I remain confident we should be able to preserve.

Please do let me know if you would like to discuss this further by telephone at a time that suits you.

With kind regards,
Jonathan Fairweather

Dear Dr. and Dr. Ashworth,

I am delighted to write to you at this rather exciting juncture, as we are now very close indeed to being in a position to exchange contracts on your new home, and I wanted to take a moment to explain clearly what the exchange process involves and what you can expect over the next few days, so that when the moment arrives it feels entirely familiar rather than in any way alarming.

Exchange of contracts is, in essence, the point at which the transaction becomes legally binding on both parties — before exchange, either side may withdraw without legal consequence, whereas after exchange you and the seller are each committed to completing on the agreed date, which is a significant and genuinely positive milestone — and it is achieved by the two sets of solicitors reading out and confirming the terms of the contract to one another by telephone in a manner prescribed by the Law Society, following which both signed contracts are treated as having been exchanged simultaneously.

In order to reach that point, I will need to confirm a few remaining matters with you: specifically, I would ask that you review and sign the contract in the form I enclosed with my earlier report, that you confirm you are satisfied with the completion date of the fourteenth, and that you ensure your deposit funds — which as we discussed will amount to ten percent of the purchase price — are transferred to our client account in cleared funds no later than the day before we anticipate exchanging, so that there is no last-minute pressure.

Once exchange has taken place, I will call you immediately so that you hear the news directly from me, and we will then turn our attention to the completion preparations, which are generally straightforward and which I will guide you through in the same step-by-step manner we have followed throughout.

I do want to say that it has been a genuine pleasure acting for you both, and I very much look forward to getting you across the finishing line.

With warmest regards,
Jonathan Fairweather

meta.name: Priya Nambiar
meta.title: Partner, Real Estate – Commercial Property & Development

lawyers.summary: Priya advises developers, landlords, and institutional investors on the acquisition, disposal, and financing of commercial real estate assets. Her practice covers planning agreements, option contracts, and complex multi-title assemblies for large-scale development sites. She coordinates carefully with planning counsel and local authority teams to ensure that conditions precedent are satisfied in the correct sequence. Clients with mixed-use schemes, permitted development conversions, or ground-lease structures will find her process-driven approach keeps transactions on programme.
corpus:
Dear Mr. Kellerman,

I wanted to reach out today to walk you through where things stand with the due diligence process on the Hartfield Road acquisition, and to reassure you that, while there are a number of moving parts at this stage, each of them is entirely routine and well within what we would typically expect to manage on a transaction of this nature.

Our team has now received the title search results from the vendor's solicitors, and we are working through those carefully to confirm that the title is clean and free of any encumbrances that might give us cause for concern. There is one notation relating to a historical easement on the eastern boundary of the lot which, at first glance, may look a little unsettling, but I would ask you not to worry unduly at this point — easements of this kind are generally a standard feature of older commercial titles, and we should be able to confirm within the next few days whether it materially affects your intended use of the site.

In the meantime, I would encourage you to continue your conversations with your financing team, as we will want to have the facility documentation ready to move forward promptly once we have cleared the title issues. I will be in touch again as soon as I have something more definitive to report, and please do not hesitate to call me if you have any questions in the interim.

Kind regards,
Priya

Dear Ms. Okonkwo,

Thank you so much for making the time to speak with me yesterday — it was genuinely helpful to hear your vision for the development in more detail, and I wanted to follow up in writing so that you have a clear sense of the steps we will be working through together over the coming weeks.

As we discussed, the rezoning application is typically the first and, I will be candid with you, often the most time-intensive part of a project of this scale, simply because it involves a degree of dialogue with the local council that can move at its own pace. What I would reassure you of, however, is that the grounds on which we are applying are well-supported by the strategic planning framework currently in place for that corridor, and we generally find that applications of this type, when carefully prepared, are received constructively by the planning authority.

Over the next fortnight, my team will be preparing the planning statement and coordinating the supporting technical reports — traffic, environmental, and urban design — which will need to accompany the application. I will circulate a draft of the planning statement to you for your review before anything is lodged, so that you have a full opportunity to ensure it accurately reflects your intentions for the site. Each of these steps is normal and manageable, and we will guide you through all of them with as little disruption to your time as possible.

Please feel free to reach out at any time if anything comes to mind.

Warm regards,
Priya

Dear Mr. and Ms. Thornton,

I hope this message finds you both well, and I wanted to take a moment to update you on the progress of the commercial lease negotiation with your prospective tenant, as I know the timeline has been weighing on your minds a little.

I am pleased to report that we have now exchanged revised heads of terms with the tenant's solicitors, and the points of difference between the parties are narrowing quite meaningfully. There are still a handful of matters outstanding — most notably the rent review mechanism and the make-good provisions at the end of the term — but these are, I would assure you, entirely standard negotiating points in a commercial lease of this length and value, and we are well-positioned to reach an outcome that protects your interests as landlord.

What I would suggest at this stage is that we schedule a brief call early next week so that I can walk you through the tenant's latest position on those outstanding clauses and take your instructions before I respond. It is generally useful, at this point in a negotiation, to have a clear sense of where you are willing to show some flexibility and where you would prefer to hold firm, and that conversation will allow me to negotiate on your behalf with a great deal more precision.

I will have my assistant reach out to find a time that suits you both, and in the meantime, please do not hesitate to contact me directly if anything arises.

Warm regards,
Priya

Dear Dr. Vasquez,

I wanted to write to you today with a calm and thorough overview of what the settlement process will look like from here, because I find that clients often feel considerably more at ease once they understand exactly what is happening and in what order — and I am confident that, in your case, there is every reason to feel reassured.

Now that contracts have been exchanged on the Meridian Street premises, we have moved into what is typically called the settlement period, during which a number of administrative and financial steps are completed in a careful, sequenced way. Our immediate priorities are to attend to the stamping of the transfer documents, to liaise with your lender's solicitors to ensure that the mortgage documentation is in order, and to conduct the pre-settlement searches, which will confirm that nothing has changed on the title since exchange — a step that is entirely routine but one we treat with great care nonetheless.

Approximately one week before the scheduled settlement date, I will send you a detailed settlement statement setting out the precise figures that will need to be in place, and we will walk through those numbers together so that there are no surprises on the day itself. Settlement, when everything is prepared properly, is generally a smooth and almost anticlimactic event — which is exactly how we like it.

Please feel free to call or email me at any point if you have questions, and I look forward to guiding you through to a successful completion.

Warm regards,
Priya

Dear Ms. Fujiwara,

Thank you for bringing the strata title dispute to my attention, and I want to begin by saying that, while I understand this situation has been a source of considerable frustration for you and your co-investors, matters of this kind — while genuinely inconvenient — are generally more resolvable than they first appear, and I would encourage you to approach the process ahead with a measure of optimism.

From what you have described, the core issue relates to a disagreement between your lot owners' corporation and the developer over the allocation of certain common property maintenance obligations, and this is, I would say, a fairly well-trodden area of dispute in commercial strata developments. The legislation governing these relationships is reasonably clear on the relevant obligations, and we should be able to form a confident view, once we have reviewed the strata plan and the by-laws in detail, as to where the balance of legal responsibility sits.

As a first step, I would ask you to gather and send through to me any correspondence you have had with the developer or the strata manager over the past twelve months, along with a copy of the strata plan and the most recent meeting minutes of the owners' corporation, if you have those to hand. With those documents before us, we will be in a much stronger position to assess your options, which may range from a formal letter of demand through to mediation or, if necessary, an application to the relevant tribunal — though in my experience, a well-constructed letter of demand resolves the majority of disputes of this nature before they progress further.

I am here to guide you through each step of this, and I will be in touch again once I have had a chance to review the materials you send through.

Warm regards,
Priya

meta.name: Sebastian Holt
meta.title: Partner, Commercial – Mergers, Acquisitions & Corporate Finance

lawyers.summary: Sebastian advises buyers, sellers, and management teams on the full transaction cycle of mergers and acquisitions, from heads of terms through to post-completion adjustment mechanisms. He structures deals with close attention to valuation metrics, earn-out formulae, and warranty and indemnity insurance thresholds. In the last three years his team has closed 23 transactions with an aggregate deal value exceeding £1.4 billion. Mandates typically involve private equity sponsors, founder-led exits, or secondary buyouts where pricing precision is non-negotiable.
corpus:
Dear Mr Ashworth,

I write further to our call of 14 March and in connection with the proposed acquisition by Thornfield Capital Partners Ltd ("Thornfield") of the entire issued share capital of Meridian Logistics Group plc ("Target") for an aggregate Consideration of £47.3 million, structured as to £38.5 million in cash and £8.8 million in deferred consideration contingent upon satisfaction of the Revenue Earn-Out Conditions set out in Schedule 4 of the draft Share Purchase Agreement ("SPA") circulated on 11 March ("Draft SPA").

Having reviewed the Target's disclosure letter dated 8 March, I flag three matters requiring your instructions before we can progress to execution. First, the warranty at Clause 12.4(b) of the Draft SPA concerning the absence of material litigation is qualified only by disclosures of proceedings with an aggregate claim value below £250,000; the Employment Tribunal claim filed on 27 February by four former warehouse operatives, which counsel has valued at up to £410,000 inclusive of costs, falls outside that threshold and must be addressed either by upward revision of the disclosure basket or by a specific indemnity in Thornfield's favour. Second, the Locked Box Accounts date of 31 December 2024 creates a leakage exposure window of approximately 103 days to the anticipated Completion Date of 13 April; I strongly recommend a specific leakage covenant accompanied by daily interest at a rate of no less than SONIA plus 2.5% on any Permitted Leakage amounts exceeding £75,000. Third, the condition precedent at Clause 5.1(c) requiring Phase 1 environmental clearance on the Coventry distribution facility has no long-stop date; I propose inserting a long-stop of 60 days from exchange, with automatic termination rights in favour of Thornfield thereafter.

Please revert with your instructions on the above no later than close of business on 18 March so that we may circulate a revised mark-up to the Sellers' solicitors, Hendricks & Vane LLP, within the agreed timetable.

Yours sincerely,
Sebastian Holt

Dear Ms Okafor,

I write in connection with the proposed Series B financing of Arcturus Technologies Ltd ("Company") and the term sheet ("Term Sheet") issued by Vantage Growth Fund III on 4 April 2025, pursuant to which Vantage proposes to subscribe for 18,750,000 new Series B Preferred Shares at a pre-money valuation of £24 million, representing an aggregate investment of £6 million and a post-money dilution to existing Ordinary shareholders of approximately 20.1%.

I draw your attention to three provisions in the Term Sheet which, in my assessment, are commercially adverse to the Company and its founders and which I recommend you instruct me to negotiate prior to executing any binding documentation. First, the full-ratchet anti-dilution protection proposed at paragraph 8 is the most aggressive formulation available and would, on any future down-round, reset Vantage's conversion price to the lower issuance price in its entirety without regard to the quantum of diluting shares issued; I would strongly advocate for broad-based weighted average anti-dilution as the market-standard position, which in practical terms would substantially reduce the dilutive impact on the founders' holdings in a downside scenario. Second, the drag-along threshold at paragraph 14 permits Vantage, acting alone as a Series B majority holder, to drag all shareholders on a sale at any price exceeding 1.0x the Series B Liquidation Preference; this is below the protection I would ordinarily regard as acceptable, and I propose a minimum drag threshold of no less than 1.5x the Series B Issue Price. Third, the information rights covenant at paragraph 19 requires delivery of monthly management accounts within 10 Business Days of month-end; given the Company's current finance team headcount of three, I recommend negotiating this to 20 Business Days to avoid a technical breach position.

I am available to discuss the above at your convenience and would recommend a call no later than 10 April to preserve the timetable for a targeted close by 30 June 2025.

Yours sincerely,
Sebastian Holt

Dear Mr Castellano,

I write to provide a further update on the proposed demerger of Harwick Industrial Holdings plc's ("Harwick") specialty chemicals division ("ChemCo Division") into a separately listed entity, provisionally styled Harwick Specialty Chemicals plc ("NewCo"), by way of a Court-approved scheme of arrangement under Part 26 of the Companies Act 2006 ("Scheme").

Following the Board meeting of 19 March, I confirm the following critical path milestones and attendant legal requirements. The Scheme Document, which must comply with the Listing Rules (LR 13) and the Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules, is targeted for circulation to Harwick shareholders on or around 5 May 2025; we are presently 72 hours behind the internal drafting schedule as a result of the delay in receipt of the Class 1 working capital report from your reporting accountants, Brindley Moore LLP, and I urge you to escalate internally to ensure delivery by 24 March without further slippage. The FCA has indicated informally that its review period on the Prospectus will be approximately 20 Business Days from submission; on the basis of a targeted submission date of 28 March, this places FCA approval no earlier than 25 April, which in turn compresses the period available for the Scheme Court Hearing to a single viable window of 15–16 May. Any further delay to the Prospectus submission will necessitate a full timetable reset and a revised announcement to the market under the Market Abuse Regulation (UK MAR), Article 17. The tax clearance application submitted to HMRC on 3 February in respect of the proposed capital reduction forming part of the demerger structure remains outstanding at Day 49; I recommend your tax advisers, Pemberton Gale LLP, chase for a response by 21 March, as the absence of clearance by 30 April will prevent the Scheme becoming effective within the Q2 2025 window.

I will circulate a revised critical path schedule by end of day on 21 March for your review and approval.

Yours sincerely,
Sebastian Holt

Dear Ms Fernandez-Webb,

I write further to your instructions of 25 March regarding the proposed management buy-out ("MBO") of Saltire Precision Engineering Ltd ("Company") from its current shareholder, Glencroft Private Equity LLP ("Vendor"), by a management team comprising four individuals ("Management Team") to be funded through a combination of senior debt provided by Caledonian Commercial Bank plc ("Senior Lender") in the amount of £9.75 million, mezzanine finance from Oakthorn Capital in the amount of £2.25 million, and Management Team equity of £1.5 million, implying an Enterprise Value of £13.5 million and an EBITDA multiple of approximately 6.4x based on the Company's audited EBITDA of £2.11 million for the financial year ended 31 December 2024.

The Newco acquisition vehicle ("Bidco") will be incorporated as a private limited company and the capital structure will be documented by way of an Investment Agreement and Articles of Association to be negotiated between all equity and debt stakeholders. I flag at this stage that the Senior Lender's term sheet dated 22 March contains a financial covenant package requiring Bidco to maintain a minimum Interest Cover Ratio of 2.0x and a maximum Total Net Leverage Ratio of 3.5x, each tested quarterly on a trailing twelve-month basis; given the Company's projected EBITDA growth of 8% per annum over the three-year business plan, these covenants appear serviceable in the base case but provide limited headroom in a downside scenario of revenue contraction of 10% or more, which I would characterise as a material execution risk. I recommend the Management Team's financial advisers model covenant headroom under a stressed scenario before the Facilities Agreement is executed.

I propose convening a structuring call with all advisers during the week commencing 31 March to align on the Bidco structure, inter-creditor terms, and the proposed Completion mechanism. Please confirm the Management Team's availability.

Yours sincerely,
Sebastian Holt

meta.name: Claire Ashford
meta.title: Partner, Commercial – Contracts, Licensing & Supply Chain

lawyers.summary: Claire drafts and negotiates high-value commercial contracts, technology licences, and cross-border supply and distribution agreements. She benchmarks key terms—liability caps, service-level penalties, IP ownership carve-outs—against current market data before any negotiation opens. Her clients include manufacturers, SaaS vendors, and retailers whose contract portfolios carry material revenue or regulatory exposure. Where a single clause carries seven-figure risk, her figure-led review process ensures the numbers in the contract match the numbers in the board's risk register.
corpus:
Dear Mr. Hargreaves,

I write further to our call of 14 March 2024 concerning the proposed Software Licensing Agreement between Deltron Systems Limited ("Deltron") and your company, Vantage Analytics plc ("Vantage"), and specifically the unresolved position on Clause 11.4 (Audit Rights).

Deltron's current draft entitles them to conduct audits of Vantage's deployment records no fewer than twice per calendar year, with 72 hours' written notice. In my view, this provision is commercially disproportionate given the Annual Licence Fee of £840,000 and the non-exclusive, read-only nature of the Permitted Use as defined in Schedule 2. I would recommend limiting audit frequency to once per 12-month period, extending the notice period to 20 Business Days, and inserting a cap on Deltron's audit costs recoverable from Vantage to 0.5% of the Consideration in any given Contract Year.

I would also draw your attention to the absence of a materiality threshold in Clause 11.4(c): as currently drafted, any discrepancy — however de minimis — triggers Deltron's right to terminate. This requires amendment before execution.

Please confirm by close of business on 19 March 2024 whether you are content for me to submit a revised mark-up on this basis.

Yours sincerely,
Claire Ashford
Partner, Commercial – Contracts, Licensing & Supply Chain

Dear Ms. Okonkwo,

I write to advise on the force majeure position arising under Clause 19 of the Master Supply Agreement dated 7 September 2022 (the "MSA") between Fortis Manufacturing Group Limited ("Fortis") and your company, Meridian Retail Holdings plc ("Meridian"), in light of the documented port disruption affecting Fortis's principal logistics corridor since 28 February 2024.

Fortis has invoked Clause 19.1, asserting that the disruption constitutes a Force Majeure Event as defined therein. Having reviewed the supporting documentation provided on 4 March 2024, I assess the invocation as procedurally valid: notice was served within the 5-Business Day window prescribed by Clause 19.2, and the disruption does appear to fall within the enumerated category of "governmental or regulatory action affecting international freight." However, I note that Clause 19.4 imposes a 45-calendar-day cap on Force Majeure suspension, beyond which Meridian retains a right of termination without liability for the outstanding Purchase Orders, which at the date of this letter represent a combined value of approximately £3.1m.

I recommend that Meridian formally acknowledge receipt of the Clause 19.1 notice in writing, without prejudice to its position on quantum, and that we instruct an independent logistics consultant to assess the proportionality of the claimed disruption no later than 22 March 2024. This will protect Meridian's position should the matter proceed to dispute resolution under Clause 27.

Please revert at your earliest convenience so that we may coordinate next steps.

Yours sincerely,
Claire Ashford
Partner, Commercial – Contracts, Licensing & Supply Chain

Dear Mr. Castellano,

I write in connection with the proposed assignment by Nexbridge Distribution Limited ("Nexbridge") of its rights and obligations under the Exclusive Distribution Agreement dated 3 June 2021 (the "EDA") to its newly incorporated subsidiary, Nexbridge Logistics (UK) Limited ("NL-UK"), and specifically to advise Solvara Consumer Goods plc ("Solvara") on the consent mechanics and associated risk.

Clause 14.1 of the EDA prohibits assignment without Solvara's prior written consent, such consent not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed. Nexbridge has submitted a formal consent request dated 8 March 2024. Before Solvara grants consent, I would advise that three conditions be satisfied and documented: first, that NL-UK provides a parent company guarantee from Nexbridge in respect of all outstanding and future obligations under the EDA, given that NL-UK has no independently audited financial history; second, that Solvara receives certified confirmation that NL-UK holds all regulatory authorisations required under Schedule 4 of the EDA, including the relevant HMRC Excise registration; and third, that the Minimum Annual Purchase Commitment of £2.4m set out in Clause 6.3 is expressly assumed by NL-UK in the executed Assignment Deed, with no reduction or deferral.

I would caution against granting consent unconditionally or by conduct. If Solvara proceeds on any lesser basis, the enforceability of the Minimum Annual Purchase Commitment as against NL-UK becomes materially uncertain.

I am available to discuss at your convenience and can circulate a draft consent letter incorporating the above conditions by 21 March 2024, subject to your instruction.

Yours sincerely,
Claire Ashford
Partner, Commercial – Contracts, Licensing & Supply Chain

Dear Ms. Fairweather,

I write to provide a preliminary assessment of the intellectual property ownership provisions in the draft Co-Development and Commercialisation Agreement (the "CDCA") circulated by Halcyon Biotech Limited ("Halcyon") on 11 March 2024, and to flag certain provisions that, in my view, require material revision before Praxis Ingredients plc ("Praxis") can proceed to execution.

Clause 8.2 of the CDCA purports to vest ownership of all Foreground IP — defined in Clause 1.1 as intellectual property created in the course of the Development Programme — in Halcyon absolutely, with Praxis receiving only a non-exclusive, royalty-bearing licence at a rate of 4.5% of Net Sales Revenue as defined in Schedule 3. This is commercially disadvantageous to Praxis in circumstances where, per Annex B of the CDCA, Praxis is contributing 60% of the Development Costs, which are projected at £5.8m over the 36-month Development Period. A contribution of that magnitude warrants either co-ownership of Foreground IP or, at minimum, an exclusive licence in respect of Praxis's designated field of use as set out in Schedule 1, Part II.

Further, Clause 8.5 contains no carve-out for Background IP brought to the programme by Praxis. As currently drafted, improvements to Praxis's Background IP that arise during the Development Programme would fall within the definition of Foreground IP and vest in Halcyon. This is unacceptable and must be corrected as a condition of Praxis's agreement to proceed.

I propose to submit a comprehensive mark-up of Clause 8 by 25 March 2024. Please confirm that you are content for me to proceed on that timetable.

Yours sincerely,
Claire Ashford
Partner, Commercial – Contracts, Licensing & Supply Chain

Dear Mr. Tindall,

I write further to your instructions of 12 March 2024 concerning the proposed termination by Caldwell Procurement Services Limited ("Caldwell") of the Framework Supply Agreement dated 15 January 2020 (the "FSA") with Orion Industrial Components plc ("Orion"), and to advise on the contractual and financial exposure arising therefrom.

Clause 22.3 of the FSA entitles Caldwell to terminate for convenience upon 90 calendar days' written notice. However, Clause 22.5 provides that, in the event of termination for convenience during the Initial Term — which, per Clause 3.1, expires on 14 January 2025 — Caldwell shall pay Orion a Termination Payment equal to 15% of the aggregate value of Call-Off Orders placed in the preceding 12-month period. Based on the Order Schedule provided on 6 March 2024, that figure is approximately £4.3m, yielding a Termination Payment liability of approximately £645,000. I would ask that your finance team verify this calculation against your internal records before any notice is served.

I would also note that Clause 22.5 is silent on whether the Termination Payment is inclusive or exclusive of VAT. Given the sums involved, this ambiguity should be clarified with Orion in writing prior to service of notice, to avoid a subsequent dispute as to the amount due.

Finally, Caldwell should be aware that service of a termination notice will trigger Orion's step-in rights under Clause 18.4 in respect of three active sub-supply contracts. I recommend we review those sub-contracts in parallel before the notice is issued.

Please confirm your instructions and I will prepare a draft termination notice accordingly.

Yours sincerely,
Claire Ashford
Partner, Commercial – Contracts, Licensing & Supply Chain
